title: Multi-Channel Notification Pipeline
spec:
## Requirements

### R1: Notification Routing
Route notifications to the correct channel(s) based on user preferences and notification type. Channels: email (SendGrid), push (Firebase), SMS (Twilio), in-app (WebSocket). A single notification event can trigger multiple channels.

### R2: Template Rendering
Each channel has its own template format. Email = HTML, Push = title+body (150 char limit), SMS = plain text (160 char limit), In-app = JSON payload. Templates support variable interpolation from notification data.

### R3: Rate Limiting
Limit notifications per user per channel: email max 10/hour, push max 20/hour, SMS max 5/hour, in-app unlimited. Rate limit state in Redis.

### R4: Delivery Tracking
Track delivery status per notification per channel. States: queued, sent, delivered, failed, bounced. Status updates arrive via webhooks from each provider.

### R5: Retry Logic
Failed deliveries retry with exponential backoff: 1min, 5min, 30min, then dead-letter. Max 3 retries per channel per notification.

### R6: Preference Management
Users configure: which channels they want, quiet hours (no notifications during specified time windows), notification type opt-outs.

## Data Flow
NotificationEvent → RoutingDecision (channels[]) → per-channel: TemplateRender → RateLimitCheck → Deliver → TrackStatus
Webhook → StatusUpdate → RetryDecision?

## Build Sequence
1. R1 (Routing) + R6 (Preferences) — foundation
2. R2 (Templates) — depends on R1
3. R3 (Rate Limiting) — depends on R1
4. R4 (Tracking) + R5 (Retry) — depends on delivery

## Architecture
PostgreSQL for preferences, notification log, templates. Redis for rate limit counters. Event-driven: notification events trigger the pipeline. Each channel adapter is independent.
rejection:
## Anti-Patterns

### Do NOT couple channel adapters
Each channel (email, push, SMS, in-app) is a separate adapter_out concern. Adding a new channel must not touch existing ones.

### Do NOT block on delivery
The notification pipeline is async. The caller fires an event and does not wait for delivery confirmation.

### Do NOT put routing logic in adapters
Adapters deliver. The routing policy decides WHERE to deliver. These are separate concerns.

### Do NOT hardcode rate limits
Rate limits are configuration, not code. They must be changeable without deployment.

## Boundaries
- Routing is a policy (decides which channels)
- Template rendering is a transformer (data → formatted output)
- Each channel adapter is an adapter_out (internal → external)
- Rate limiting is a validator (pass/fail gate)
- Delivery tracking is a command (writes status)
- Retry logic is a policy (decides whether to retry)
- Preferences is a query (reads user config)

## Drift Risks
- Routing logic migrating into individual adapters
- Rate limiting becoming per-adapter instead of centralized
- Template rendering merging with delivery (should be separate: render then deliver)
additionalConstraints: Each channel adapter must be independently deployable
